plumage.underparts: Rusty-orange (cinnamon-rust; deeper in males, paler in females)
plumage.eye_line: Bold black stripe from bill through eye to nape
plumage.crown: Dark gray-black (darker in males, grayer in females)
plumage.bill: Long, straight, slightly upturned -- precision bark probe

secondary_calls.name: Ank contact call
secondary_calls.description: Softer, shorter single-note version of yank

interspecies_network.mechanism: Extracts threat-level information from chickadee D-note alarm calls
interspecies_network.behavior_response: Increased vigilance and altered foraging when exposed to chickadee calls with more D notes (higher-threat predators)

theory_nodes.concept: Metronomic pulse (steady beat as temporal foundation)
examples: Identical notes at a steady rate of 2-3 per second -- the temporal grid of the canopy
In a studio, the click track is the first thing laid down; all instruments sync to it
The nuthatch's yank-yank functions as a temporal landmark in forest recordings
No variation in pitch, duration, timbre, or amplitude -- pure temporal reference
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The metronome / click track -- the steady beat that provides the temporal grid against which all other rhythms are measured. Not a melody, not an improvisation, not a chord -- pure temporal reference. The hi-hat that holds the groove.

examples: Fundamental frequency with strong, closely spaced overtones creating buzzy quality
High harmonic-to-fundamental ratio -- many overtones contributing significant energy
Brass-like: oboe, muted trumpet, French horn -- instruments that cut through orchestral texture
Rich harmonic content activates wider spectral band, improving audibility against broadband noise
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Brass-like timbre -- the spectral signature of instruments that cut through texture. The nasal quality is not a deficiency; it is a penetration strategy. A pure tone would be masked by canopy noise. The rich harmonic stack of the yank call ensures audibility.

examples: Recognizable through consistency, not complexity or meaning
The same note, same tempo, same timbre, repeated -- identity through never changing
In music, the most memorable hooks are often the simplest: a repeated riff, a persistent bass line
The wren is known by complexity; the chickadee by meaning; the nuthatch by consistency
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Identity through invariance -- being recognizable because you never change. The groove that holds. The reliable presence that everyone synchronizes to without noticing until it stops.

theory_nodes.concept: Inverted perspective as retrograde (direction-reversal as discovery)
examples: Head-first trunk descent = same sequence read in reverse direction
Retrograde in counterpoint reveals hidden structures invisible in forward playback
Inverted foraging reveals hidden food in bark crevices invisible from ascending perspective
Both are direction-reversal applied to the same substrate -- different viewing angle, different discoveries
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Retrograde -- playing a musical sequence in reverse. The same notes, same intervals, but read right-to-left. A Bach fugue subject and its retrograde contain the same material but different contours. The nuthatch's head-first descent and the ascending creeper see the same bark from opposite directions.

examples: Confirmed receiver of chickadee D-note alarm broadcast (Templeton & Greene 2007)
Extracts threat-level information -- not just responding to alarm presence but decoding content
First cross-degree dependency in the species engine: degree 5 functionally depends on degree 3
The network graph gains its first directed edge: chickadee -> nuthatch
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The interspecies network is a communication bus -- the chickadee broadcasts, the nuthatch receives and acts on the encoded information. Like a bandleader's cue system: one player signals, others respond with appropriate changes in their own performance.

theory_nodes.concept: Ostinato bass (pure single-note repetition as harmonic/temporal anchor)
examples: Simplest form of ostinato: one note, repeated (vs. owl's 4-note motif ostinato at degree 4)
Pachelbel's Canon, Purcell's Dido's Lament -- repeated bass line anchoring upper voices
The basso ostinato of the conifer canopy: steady pulse while wren, thrush, chickadee elaborate above
Ground bass tradition: harmonic/temporal anchor that does not change while everything else does
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Basso ostinato / ground bass -- the simplest possible repeated figure, providing the harmonic and temporal foundation over which other voices elaborate. The owl's ostinato had internal structure (4 notes). The nuthatch's is reduced to essence: one note, repeated.

cross_references.title: Wings of the Pacific Northwest
cross_references.connection: Passerine taxonomy and distribution; this release adds metronome analysis, eavesdropping network node, and inverted-perspective ecology

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: Conifer forest ecology, seed caching/dispersal, insect pest regulation, mixed-species flock dynamics

cross_references.title: BPS Sensor Network
cross_references.connection: Steady temporal signal maps to BPS clock synchronization concepts. Metronomic natural pulse as reference clock for acoustic monitoring.

cross_references.title: Seattle 360 Music
cross_references.connection: Part A parallel: Overton Berry at degree 5 -- both are persistent, reliable presences. Consistency as defining trait.

cross_references.title: Black-capped Chickadee (Degree 3)
cross_references.connection: Interspecies eavesdropping network. Nuthatch is confirmed client of chickadee alarm broadcast (Templeton & Greene 2007). First directed edge in the network graph: chickadee -> nuthatch.

cross_references.title: Great Blue Heron (Degree 0)
cross_references.connection: Heron's silence is the blank canvas. Nuthatch's steady beat is the temporal grid drawn on that canvas. Six degrees in, the forest has both silence and pulse.

cross_references.title: Pacific Wren (Degree 1)
cross_references.connection: Both are small year-round conifer forest residents. Wren's 100+ note cascade vs. nuthatch's single-note repetition: maximum variety vs. maximum consistency. Both instantly recognized -- wren by complexity, nuthatch by simplicity.

cross_references.title: Varied Thrush (Degree 2)
cross_references.connection: Both occupy conifer canopy. Thrush sustains one tone; nuthatch repeats one note. Sustain vs. pulse. AM vs. metronomic. Minimalist aesthetic in both cases.

cross_references.title: Northern Spotted Owl (Degree 4)
cross_references.connection: Direct predecessor. Owl introduced rhythm (4-note motif with internal structure). Nuthatch reduces rhythm to essence: one note, steady beat. The motif has complexity; the metronome has purity. Both contribute to temporal structure; they serve different functions.

part_a_parallel.shared_principle: Both are persistent, reliable presences -- Berry at the piano in Seattle's jazz clubs for decades, the nuthatch on the conifer trunks year after year. Consistency as defining trait. Neither is the flashiest voice in the room, but both are the pulse that holds the ensemble together.
resonance_points: Persistent presence: Berry played Seattle jazz clubs for over 50 years, always there, always dependable; the nuthatch is a year-round resident always calling from the conifers -- both define themselves through sustained reliability over time

Steady pulse: Berry's piano style was groove-centered -- laying down the rhythmic and harmonic foundation for other players to solo over; the nuthatch's metronomic yank-yank provides the temporal grid for the canopy acoustic ensemble

Unflashy excellence: Berry was not the loudest or most virtuosic player -- he was the one everyone wanted on the bandstand because he held things together; the nuthatch is not the most complex singer -- it is the most reliable

Consistency as identity: Berry was recognizable by his presence, his dependability, his decades of showing up; the nuthatch is recognizable by never changing its call -- same note, same tempo, same timbre, year after year

head_first_descent.description: Descends tree trunks head-first -- unique among North American bark-foraging birds
head_first_descent.biomechanics: Alternating foot-hold system; long hind claw (hallux) hooks above center of gravity; body rotates around gripping foot
head_first_descent.ecological_advantage: Inverted perspective reveals insects, spiders, and seeds in bark crevices invisible from ascending approach angle
head_first_descent.music_theory_analog: Retrograde -- same sequence, opposite direction. Direction-reversal as discovery technique.

resin_defense.description: Smears conifer resin (pitch) around nesting cavity entrance
resin_defense.agents: Both male and female apply resin, carrying it on bills from fresh wounds on nearby conifers
resin_defense.function: Sticky barrier deters nest predators (squirrels, other cavity-nesters) and nest parasites
resin_defense.entry_technique: Dives straight through resin ring without touching it -- precision maneuver

degree_arc.position: The metronome -- steady beat as temporal foundation
degree_arc.progression: Silence (0) -> cascade complexity (1) -> sustained tone (2) -> referential syntax (3) -> rhythmic motif (4) -> steady beat (5)
degree_arc.key_insight: Degree 5 completes a critical gap in the ensemble: the PULSE. Degrees 0-4 established silence, melody, harmony, language, and motif. The nuthatch provides the temporal grid -- the click track against which all other rhythms are located. The ensemble now has both rhythmic figure (owl's motif) and rhythmic ground (nuthatch's metronome). Additionally, degree 5 establishes the first cross-degree dependency through the interspecies eavesdropping network (chickadee -> nuthatch), making the species engine a graph rather than a list.

synthesis_types.degree_4: Rhythmic cell (motif -- 4 notes with internal temporal structure)
synthesis_types.degree_5: Metronomic pulse (single note, steady rate -- pure temporal reference)

register_progression.degree_5: Mid-range (1.5-3.5 kHz fundamental, harmonics to 4+ kHz)

content_progression.degree_1: Identity + fitness (who is singing, how well)
content_progression.degree_3: Referential threat information (what is there, how dangerous)
content_progression.degree_4: Territorial identity through rhythm (I am here, this is my pattern, this is my time)
content_progression.degree_5: Temporal grid (the steady beat that locates all other rhythms in time)

temporal_progression.degree_4: Nocturnal (first night species -- time as territory)
temporal_progression.degree_5: Diurnal (year-round daytime pulse -- returns to daylight)

ecology.habitat_requirement: Conifer forest (mature or second-growth; not old-growth obligate)
ecology.foraging_strategy: Head-first trunk descent on conifers; omnidirectional bark-foraging
ecology.diet: Conifer seeds, insects (bark beetles, spruce budworm), spiders
ecology.seed_caching: Scatter hoarder -- caches thousands of conifer seeds in bark crevices each autumn
ecology.seed_dispersal: Forgotten caches may germinate -- minor seed dispersal agent for conifers
ecology.nesting: Excavates own cavity in dead/decaying wood; applies conifer resin around entrance
ecology.pair_bond: Monogamous; ank contact call maintains pair coordination during foraging
ecology.irruptive_migration: Year-round resident in most range; irruptive southward/downslope movement in poor cone crop years (Bock & Lepthien 1972; Koenig & Knops 2001)

conservation.trend: Stable to slightly increasing (BBS data, western Washington)
conservation.significance: Deliberately low conservation concern (contrast with degree 4 Threatened owl). Value is reliability, not rarity.

key_sites.name: Western Washington conifer forests
key_sites.description: Most abundant nuthatch in Douglas fir, western hemlock, and Sitka spruce forests

key_sites.name: Urban/suburban areas with conifers
key_sites.description: Common in neighborhoods with large conifers; visits suet and seed feeders

key_sites.name: Olympic and Cascade National Forests
key_sites.description: Common throughout conifer zones; often in mixed-species flocks with chickadees and kinglets
